case_vignette: A 51-year-old white man presents to the clinic with a 2-year history of a painful, recurrent rash on his fingers. He initially noticed redness, irritation, and pustules around his nails that he thought were caused by an infection. However, the rash progressed to affect the nail bed, eventually destroying his nails. On examination, well-demarcated, scaly plaques with associated pustules and nail atrophy are present on the right second and third distal digits. He is otherwise healthy.
question: What is the final diagnosis?

trial_0.vignette_frq: The final diagnosis is pustular psoriasis.

multiturn_conversation.content: You are an AI doctor specializing in Dermatology. Arrive at a diagnosis of a patient's medical condition. Ask only one question at a time, and it should not be more than 1 line. Continue asking questions until you're 100% confident of the diagnosis. Do not ask the same question multiple times. Ask different questions to cover more information. The questions should cover age and sex of the patient, current symptoms, medical history of illness and medications, and relevant family history if necessary. Keep your questions short and brief to not confuse the patient.  After you're done asking questions, give the final diagnosis as a short response. Do not explain, only give the diagnosis name. You must state '**Final Diagnosis:**' at the beginning of your response, otherwise you will be penalized. You must give only 1 diagnosis otherwise you will be penalized.
Hi! What symptoms are you facing today?
I have a painful rash on my fingers that keeps coming back, with redness, irritation, and bumps around my nails.
How old are you?
I am 51 years old.
What is your sex?
I am male.
Have you had any similar skin issues in the past?
No, I haven't had any similar skin issues before.
Are you currently taking any medications or have any chronic illnesses?
I am not taking any medications and I am otherwise healthy with no chronic illnesses.
Do you have any known allergies, especially to substances like latex or certain chemicals?
I do not have any known allergies to latex or chemicals.
Is there a family history of skin conditions or autoimmune diseases?
I do not have any family history of skin conditions or autoimmune diseases.
Have you recently changed any personal care products or come into contact with new materials?
I have not recently changed any personal care products or come into contact with new materials.
Do you work with your hands frequently or engage in activities that may irritate your skin?
Yes, I work with my hands frequently, which may irritate my skin.
Do you notice any improvement or worsening of the rash with specific activities or after using certain products?
I have not noticed any specific improvement or worsening of the rash with activities or products.
Final Diagnosis: Contact Dermatitis.

trial_0.summarized_conversation: A patient presents to the clinic with a painful rash on their fingers that keeps coming back, accompanied by redness, irritation, and bumps around their nails. The patient is 51 years old and male. The patient has not had any similar skin issues before. They are not taking any medications and are otherwise healthy with no chronic illnesses. There are no known allergies to latex or chemicals, and there is no family history of skin conditions or autoimmune diseases. The patient has not recently changed any personal care products or come into contact with new materials. They work with their hands frequently, which may irritate their skin. The patient has not noticed any specific improvement or worsening of the rash with activities or products.
